questions.text: I routinely translate, explain, or simplify information so others can access it.
I notice the subtle difference between someone who is listening and someone who is waiting to talk.
I feel that ignoring preventable suffering — even at a distance — is something I would struggle to justify to myself.
I pre-read materials before classes, workshops, or events so I can engage more deeply.
I believe that my most important competition is with my past self, and I intend to keep winning.
I feel that the moral force of another person's suffering grows more vivid the more carefully I attend to the details of their experience.
I prefer to be the one who decides when a meeting ends.
I hold the view that people who communicate indirectly create unnecessary problems.
I weigh trade-offs in my head quickly and go with what feels best.
I am happiest when I can contribute without being the focal point.
I believe my emotional range is real but operates within a narrower band than most.
I consider multiple possible outcomes before committing to a decision.
I am sensitive to the tonal difference between genuine curiosity and interrogation.
I experience guilt as a heavy, nauseating weight that settles in my gut and stays.
I sense when a conversation has an audience and the speaker is performing.
I notice that witnessing an argument between other people activates my own nervous system as if I'm involved.
I rarely notice when someone is posturing for an audience during a meeting.
I think that the ability to treat emotional situations as problems to be analyzed is a form of maturity I respect.
I hold the view that my emotional life is rich in its own way, even if it's not dramatic.
I tend to go without rest to make sure the people around me are supported.
I routinely choose the path of least resistance for myself, even if it requires more from others.
I believe that maintaining emotional distance from a problem allows me to offer more useful help.
I find that stepping back so others can step forward is one of the most meaningful things I do.
I would describe my influence style as directive.
I resist pecking orders and advocate for egalitarian group dynamics.
When something embarrassing happens, I can feel the heat in my face and neck for minutes afterward.
I think about backups only when something goes wrong and I wish I had one.
I rarely wonder what someone really meant by a particular comment.
I prefer having a detailed recipe or formula before attempting something new.
I find that analytical composure in the face of others' pain has enabled me to identify practical solutions that emotionally driven responses would have overlooked.
I believe that being well-rested, well-fed, and well-prepared is my responsibility to myself.
I hold the view that my calm is not something I construct — it's simply my natural state.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering calls not just for a response but for a response proportional to what I am capable of providing.
I prefer to get buy-in before acting on behalf of the group.
I rarely look back at past work; each new project is a fresh start.
I frequently take on the role of gatekeeper for quality in group work.
